title: ProtocolLab Secure DNS Contract Architecture
purpose: Describe bounded shared DNS wire semantics, strict and interoperability secure-DNS bindings, isolated recursive-resolver diagnostics, and classic UDP/TCP diagnostic calibration.
design_summary: The strict v1 TLS profile remains unchanged for exact diagnostic cohorts. A separate v2 interoperability profile keeps authenticated identity and exact transport bindings but negotiates modern RFC-compatible cryptography and records every effective parameter. Four authoritative A-query scenarios expose that profile across DoT, DoH2, DoH3, and DoQ. Interoperability-profile DoH POST accepts an omitted response Cache-Control field while retaining exact media type, status, and DNS semantics; strict-profile DoH keeps the exact no-store requirement. Two additional diagnostic scenarios model a selected recursive resolver over DoT and DoH2 with a cold cache and a runner-provided local fixture authority as the only upstream. The bounded semantic corpus, representative DoH3 semantic breadth, and classic UDP/TCP calibration remain unchanged.
key_components: bounded canonical DNS wire fixture corpus
authoritative and recursive DNS wire fixture identities
strict secure DNS TLS profile v1
secure DNS interoperability TLS profile v2
authoritative DoT, DoH2, DoH3, and DoQ interoperability scenarios
recursive-resolver DoT and DoH2 diagnostic scenarios
data_and_state_considerations: Authoritative targets answer the local plab.test. zone with RD and RA clear, AA set, no external upstream, cache disabled, and TTL zero. Recursive-resolver targets receive RD-set queries, return RA-set and AA-clear responses, flush cache before each measured operation, and reach only the local fixture authority. DoH2, DoH3, and DoQ use message ID zero. DoT uses IDs unique among outstanding queries on a TLS connection and sets the ID to zero before canonical hashing. Responses are parsed and canonical-reserialized before length and hash comparison, so alternate legal compression does not fail raw-wire equality.
edge_cases_and_constraints: DoT, DoQ, and classic TCP include the RFC-required two-octet network-order DNS message length prefix; that transport framing is excluded from the shared bare-message hash.
DoH POST uses the unencoded bare application/dns-message body; DoH GET uses unpadded base64url in the dns query parameter and no request body.
DoT allows pipelining and correlates out-of-order responses by message ID plus question.
DoQ requires one transaction per client-initiated bidirectional stream, FIN in both directions, and RFC 9250 error codes.
The v2 interoperability profile does not weaken server authentication, ALPN, transport version, fallback, or evidence requirements; it relaxes only preselected cryptographic and leaf-key identity.
DoT and DoH2 interoperability can negotiate TLS 1.2 or TLS 1.3; DoH3 and DoQ remain TLS 1.3 only.
Strict-profile, interoperability-profile, authoritative-role, and recursive-resolver-role results are distinct cohorts.
Resolver diagnostics require a controllable cache and local upstream proof, so public or externally managed resolvers are unsupported.
Negative fixtures include the authoritative SOA while preserving zero cache TTL.
The DNSSEC-shaped fixture exercises size and record parsing but explicitly does not claim cryptographic signature validity.
UDP truncation-to-TCP retry is diagnostic and is not an allowed secure-transport fallback.
HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 DoH results are separate comparison groups.
DoQ is a DNS binding over QUIC, not raw QUIC transport throughput.
alternatives_considered: Weakening the existing exact v1 scenarios was rejected because it would collapse strict and portable evidence cohorts.
A single unconstrained TLS profile was rejected because absence of negotiated-parameter evidence would make results irreproducible.
Public recursive resolvers were rejected because cache and upstream state cannot be isolated.
Resolver-role DoH3 and DoQ were deferred from the first portability slice until independent implementations and cache-control proof can be exercised coherently.
One generic secure-DNS lane was rejected because transport and role bindings are not comparable by default.
risks: Resolver cache flushing can dominate a diagnostic operation and must not be interpreted as steady-state throughput.
Negotiated cryptographic diversity fragments comparison cohorts unless effective parameters are recorded and matched.
Comparing raw response bytes would reject semantically equivalent compression choices.
HTTP or QUIC connection reuse can hide handshake cost unless requested and effective reuse are recorded.
